questions.question: Explain the components of average disk access time and state which component typically contributes the most.
keyPoints: Average access time = Average seek time + Average rotational delay + Transfer time
Seek time is typically the largest component (1-10ms)
Rotational delay is typically half a rotation (2-5ms)
Transfer time depends on amount of data and transfer rate

questions.question: Compare FCFS and SSTF disk scheduling algorithms with respect to performance and drawbacks.
keyPoints: FCFS processes requests in arrival order - simple but often inefficient due to excessive seek time
SSTF selects nearest request to minimize seek time between consecutive requests
SSTF provides better performance than FCFS
SSTF drawback: starvation for requests far from current position
SSTF is similar to SJF CPU scheduling in concept

questions.question: What is disk caching? How does it improve performance? Explain write-through and write-back caching.
keyPoints: Disk caching uses RAM to store frequently accessed disk data
Reduces access time since RAM is millions of times faster than disk
Write-through: data written to both cache and disk immediately - reliable but slower
Write-back: data written to cache first, written to disk later - faster but risk of data loss
Cache uses replacement algorithms like LRU to manage contents

questions.question: Describe the SCAN disk scheduling algorithm. Why is it called the elevator algorithm?
keyPoints: SCAN moves the disk arm from one end of the disk to the other
Services all requests along the path in order of track number
Then reverses direction and services remaining requests
Called elevator algorithm because it behaves like an elevator - goes one direction servicing all 'floors', then reverses
Provides good throughput and relatively uniform wait times

questions.question: Explain the difference between low-level (physical) formatting and logical formatting of a disk.
keyPoints: Low-level formatting divides disk into sectors with headers, ECC, and gaps - done at factory or for repartitioning
Creates fundamental sector structure on raw disk
Logical formatting creates a file system - FAT, inode table, directory entries
Creates data structures needed for file management
Initializes superblock, free space management, and root directory

questions.question: What is disk partitioning? Explain the purpose of the Master Boot Record (MBR).
keyPoints: Partitioning divides a physical disk into independent logical sections
Each partition appears as separate logical disk to OS
Allows multiple operating systems and different file systems
MBR is stored in first sector of disk
Contains bootstrap loader program to start OS
Contains partition table defining disk divisions
Maximum 4 primary partitions in traditional MBR

questions.question: Define the following disk geometry terms: track, sector, cylinder, and platter.
keyPoints: Track: circular path on disk surface where data is recorded
Sector: wedge-shaped portion of track storing fixed data (typically 512/4096 bytes)
Platter: circular magnetic disk surface that data is recorded on (multiple in a disk assembly)
Cylinder: set of all tracks with same radius across all surfaces - allows access without arm movement

questions.question: Explain the C-SCAN disk scheduling algorithm. How does it differ from SCAN and what advantage does it provide?
keyPoints: C-SCAN (Circular SCAN) moves head from one end to other servicing requests
When reaching the end, jumps back to beginning without servicing on return
Services requests in circular order - like a carousel
Difference from SCAN: SCAN reverses direction at end and services on return, C-SCAN does not
Advantage: more uniform wait times because all tracks are treated equally
Requests at outer edges don't get better service like in SCAN
Better for systems requiring predictable response times
Drawback: may have slightly higher average seek time than SCAN

questions.question: Discuss the various disk scheduling algorithms. Compare their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: FCFS: Simple, fair, but poor performance with high seek times
SSTF: Better than FCFS, minimizes seek time, but can cause starvation
SCAN (Elevator): Good throughput, uniform wait times, but may not be optimal for emerging requests
C-SCAN: Uniform wait times, treats all tracks equally, good for heavy load
LOOK: Like SCAN but reverses at last request, more efficient than full SCAN
C-LOOK: Like C-SCAN but checks only to last request
Selection depends on workload: FCFS for low load, SCAN/C-SCAN for high load
SSTF common in modern OS as default

questions.question: Explain the process of disk formatting and boot process from MBR to loading the operating system.
keyPoints: Low-level formatting: Factory process creating sectors with headers, ECC, inter-sector gaps
Partitioning: Creating logical divisions using fdisk or similar tools
Logical formatting: Creating file system structures - superblock, FAT/inodes, directory entries
MBR contains bootstrap loader in first 446 bytes and partition table (64 bytes)
Boot process: BIOS/UEFI loads MBR, executes bootstrap loader
Bootstrap loader examines partition table to find active/bootable partition
Loads boot sector of active partition into memory
Boot sector contains OS loader which loads kernel files
Kernel initializes and transfers control to operating system

questions.question: A disk has an average seek time of 5 ms, rotates at 7200 RPM, and has a transfer rate of 80 MB/s. Calculate the average time to read a 4 KB (4096 bytes) sector.
keyPoints: Average seek time = 5 ms (given)
Rotational speed = 7200 RPM = 120 RPS
Time per rotation = 1/120 = 8.33 ms
Average rotational delay = half rotation = 8.33/2 = 4.17 ms
Transfer rate = 80 MB/s = 80 × 10^6 bytes/s
Total = 5 + 4.17 + 0.05 = 9.22 ms approximately
Seek time dominates this calculation
Note: Controller overhead and queuing time would add to this
